title: Malted milk biscuits
subtitle: British malt-flavoured shortcake biscuits
excerpt: Crisp, lightly sweet biscuits flavoured with malt extract, in the tradition of the British malted biscuit. The malt gives them a distinctive toasted grain flavour that plain shortbread does not have. Rolled thin and stamped out, they bake to a firm, snappable biscuit that keeps for two weeks in a tin.
sourceNotes: Malted milk biscuits are part of the British biscuit tradition shaped by industrial bakers from the late nineteenth century, who incorporated barley malt extract into biscuit doughs for its flavour and its ability to promote browning. The domestic equivalent uses malt extract, a thick, dark, treacle-like syrup made from malted barley, as the defining flavouring. The recipe here synthesises the established domestic formula for malt-flavoured shortcake biscuits.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled biscuits in a sealed bag. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in a tin for up to two weeks. They continue to crisp over the first two days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. Bake until pale gold at the edges and just dry on the surface. The biscuit crisps as it cools.

glossaryTerms.definition: Malt extract is a thick, dark syrup produced from malted barley, used in baking to add a toasted grain flavour and to promote browning. It is hygroscopic — it draws moisture from the air — which is why malt biscuits soften faster than plain ones if left uncovered. Measure malt extract by weight rather than volume; it is dense and clings to spoons.

content.text: The character of these biscuits comes from the

: a toasted, biscuity depth that plain shortbread does not have. The dough is rolled thin and baked firm. Keep the rolled dough cold before cutting — malt extract makes the dough slightly sticky at room temperature.

Beat the butter and sugar together until pale. Add the malt extract, golden syrup, and milk and beat until combined. Sift in the flour, bicarbonate of soda, and salt and mix to a firm dough. Shape into a disc, wrap in cling film, and refrigerate for 20 minutes.

Heat the oven to 180°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper. On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough to 3 to 4 mm thickness. Cut into rounds or rectangles of about 5 cm. Place on the lined trays and bake for 12 to 14 minutes until the edges are pale gold and the surface feels just dry when pressed. The centres will still feel slightly soft — they firm as they cool. Cool on a rack. Store in a tin once completely cool.
